lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 4 - Word Vectors and Language Models

unit.name: Count-based vectors, SVD, and GloVe
unit.description: Compares predictive word2vec-style embeddings with count-based co-occurrence methods and derives the intuition behind GloVe.
unit.summary: The lecture asks whether a more direct route to word vectors is possible by counting how often words occur in each other's contexts, using a context window to form a co-occurrence count matrix [ts=2023s]. A toy corpus produces a word-by-word matrix whose rows can be treated as vectors, but raw count vectors are huge: with a 400,000-word vocabulary, the matrix is 400,000 by 400,000 rather than vocabulary size by a modest embedding dimension [ts=2153s]. Dimensionality reduction via singular value decomposition is introduced as a way to rewrite any matrix as U, singular values, and V, then truncate smaller singular values to obtain lower-dimensional word representations [ts=2213s]. Earlier methods such as latent semantic analysis and COALS partly worked; COALS improved raw SVD by using log frequencies, ramped windows that weight closer context more, and Pearson correlations, and it also discovered linear semantic components before word2vec became famous [ts=2329s]. GloVe is introduced as Stanford's count-based model aimed at obtaining linear meaning components from count matrices [ts=2481s]. Its key intuition is that ratios of co-occurrence probabilities encode meaning differences: comparing contexts of ice and steam makes solid versus gas directions emerge, while unrelated words remain near ratio 1 [ts=2521s]. By taking logs, ratios become differences, so a log-bilinear dot-product model can make vector differences correspond to log ratios of co-occurrence probabilities, with bias and threshold details treated as secondary [ts=2648s].

key_points.text: A co-occurrence matrix counts how often each word appears in the context window of other words.
Raw co-occurrence vectors are ungainly because a large vocabulary creates a massive square matrix.
SVD factorizes a matrix into U, singular values, and V, and truncating small singular values yields low-dimensional representations.
COALS improved count-based vectors with log frequencies, ramped context windows, and correlations.
GloVe seeks vector-space linear components by using ratios of co-occurrence probabilities.
Taking logs turns probability ratios into differences, enabling a log-bilinear dot-product model.

Dimensionality reduction via singular value decomposition is introduced as a way to rewrite any matrix as U, singular values, and V, then truncate smaller singular values to obtain lower-dimensional word representations [ts=2213s]. Earlier methods such as latent semantic analysis and COALS partly worked; COALS improved raw SVD by using log frequencies, ramped windows that weight closer context more, and Pearson correlations, and it also discovered linear semantic components before word2vec became famous [ts=2329s]. GloVe is introduced as Stanford's count-based model aimed at obtaining linear meaning components from count matrices [ts=2481s]. Its key intuition is that ratios of co-occurrence probabilities encode meaning differences: comparing contexts of ice and steam makes solid versus gas directions emerge, while unrelated words remain near ratio 1 [ts=2521s]. By taking logs, ratios become differences, so a log-bilinear dot-product model can make vector differences correspond to log ratios of co-occurrence probabilities, with bias and threshold details treated as secondary [ts=2648s].

kp_catalog.name: Co-occurrence matrix word representations
kp_catalog.description: Count-based embeddings start by counting how often words occur in each other's context windows.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because co-occurrence matrix word representations clarifies count-based embeddings start by counting how often words occur in each other's context windows, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: SVD dimensionality reduction for word vectors
kp_catalog.description: SVD can reduce a large co-occurrence matrix into lower-dimensional word representations by truncating smaller singular values.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because sVD dimensionality reduction for word vectors formalizes how sVD can reduce a large co-occurrence matrix into lower-dimensional word representations by truncating smaller singular values, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: GloVe log-bilinear co-occurrence-ratio model
kp_catalog.description: GloVe uses log co-occurrence ratios so vector differences correspond to meaning differences.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gloVe log-bilinear co-occurrence-ratio model formalizes how gloVe uses log co-occurrence ratios so vector differences correspond to meaning differences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Linear semantic components and analogies
kp_catalog.description: Vector differences can encode relations, enabling analogies such as King - man + woman ≈ Queen.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because linear semantic components and analogies formalizes how vector differences can encode relations, enabling analogies such as King - man + woman ≈ Queen, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
